item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
The first image was sourced back to here, under Public Domain.

The second image could not be sourced reliably and is assumed not compliant with CC 3.0. As such, it has since been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Rotom as a skip

That just made my day! :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…..Oh fuck me, why did I have to make the connection.

Guys.

It identifies as an Attack Helicopter.

Someone send help my body is rejecting itself.

That being said, and excluding that which has been said, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought i was going to downvote this, but then i contracted a bad case of the feels after reading the logs

item.title: ...I figured something out...
item.content:encoded:
So for starts, this SCP needs more attention. It's a little confusing, yes, but it is a good, well-thought out one.

And I literally figured this out.

According to the Russian journal, it states the meteor contained unusual elements, a key one in this case being silicone. They then discovered the 'firefly'. Then they pack up, and head back to base…only to be shot down.

The Firefly is the true SCP in this case. While there is no telling what the meteor is…er…what it was, it perhaps was some sort technology of sorts. Or perhaps it was giving off some sort of radio frequency some how, be it a weird incident or some sort of property. Now, this so-called Firefly then 'found' the meteor. Where it was prior to this, we can't know. It 'absorbed' the meteor, and perhaps used it as a way of transport. Fast-forward an unknown amount of time, and it perhaps got somewhat near to Earth. While exactly how good its radar is we don't know, it is reasonable to assume it had to have at least gotten to our Solar System prior to 'detection', assuming that is not were the Firefly originates.

Fast-forward again an unknown amount of time, and we hit the year 1979. The Firefly, apparently being attracted to radio waves of sorts, 'steered' the meteor, ensuring a collision with Earth. Impact.

Anywhere from a few hours to a few weeks later, a Russian Military/Science team heads out to investigate. It seems most likely, given the time frame, that the meteor landed either in or near a Soviet Border or a country in which the USSR was either fighting directly or assisting with another country. If a USSR border, it must have taken place near a second country which was having some conflict of sorts.

This Firefly may have taken a few days before deciding to 'relocate'. It then met up with the Soviet scientists, were it was welcomed and stayed for as long as a year. HQ then requested them to pack up, and to fly home, Firefly included. It is presumed Firefly was more then willing to follow.

Firefly was onboard. What happened next, we don't know. But someone shot down the chopper, intentional or not. Based off the picture of the crash, the Hind was shot in the tail, which collapsed. With no tail rotor, the Hind was doomed. It seems it nose dived at a somewhat shallow angle, suggesting the pilot knew this and was trying to make as soft a landing as he could, as helicopter usually are not intact as well as this one was. But it wasn't enough, and they were killed. Exactly how long the next step took is unknown, but it most likely happened quickly. For one reason or another, the Firefly 'absorbed' itself into the Hind. This would explain why the 'artifact' was missing, and presumed destroyed, as there would have been no evidence of the Firefly.

It seems the Firefly, perhaps trying to adapt, reformed itself into a Hind. This would explain why the Hind never takes damage, and has no mass. The noise it makes most likely a mimicry tactic to assist in it blending in. What does it want? Who's to say. But what we do know, is it wants something. Perhaps a home were it feels safe again?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For some reason I have always found that Mil-Mi helicopter to look like some kind of one-eyed monster when viewed from the front. Still remember seeing a representation of it in a Genesis/Mega Drive game and kinda slightly freaked out by it. The picture used here just kinds of adds to the creepy factor for me here.

Haven't read the entire article, but you gain a potential upvote for the use of that model of helicopter and that picture here. I will be back after reading it.

Edit: alright, just finished reading the article. Not really sure how to feel about this. On one hand, I'm kind of freaked out by a rogue ghost helicopter that to me looks like an one-eyed monster, but on the other it's obvious it's some kind of life form that has taken on the appearance of the helicopter. My question is, why? And how? Why didn't it just remain as a ball of light after the helicopter crashed?

Changing my potential upvote to a neutral no vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This, exactly. I sometimes forget how much weird stuff goes on in this world…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My feels, little firefly! Take all my feels! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm thinking the asteroid was an egg of some energy-based space being. A space being which communicates, at least between mother and hatchling, via radio.
Poor firefly, floating towards whatever carrier signal it can find, practically radiating "are you my mommy?". Is it any wonder the little lost glow-ball imprinted like a duckling on the very first flying/floating thing it encountered?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Happy to add a little creepy to your life! UVB-76 really does have a very SCP feel to it, even filtered through wikipedia.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would just like to say that this is exactly the response I was looking for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The above link to UVB-76 was creepy, like reading a real-life SCP. Not sure it's actually related to this article, but it was neat to read anyway.

As for this, it has a real classic feel to it without feeling dated. It's a good Keter that's foreboding without being overdone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Third.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I second this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You gave me feels for a little ball of Keter light.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just to be clear, the Foundation is fully aware of the connection between the Firefly and the helicopter, but they did not post that hypothesis in the executive brief.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know that the log entries are written by a GRU-P operative, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wait wait wait… could the containment of this thing be the explanation of UVB-76?

It actually makes sense to me that the firefly and copter aren't explicitly linked, since the foundation knows so little about it. It would make any explanation they gave sound forced or pseudoscience-bullshitty. Though Willjam's suggestion above shows how it could be done without that risk.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm +1'ing this because it's written very well, however I must admit I did find it a little boring and was hoping for something more.

Also, it does seem a bit bizarre that the foundation wouldn't at least put in something along the lines of "early theories suggest that SCP-1745 and the "Firefly" are the same entity etcetc." I know it's obvious to us but still.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Really like it. The little glowy orb just reminds me of something you might find or play as in an indie video game. And now its an attack chopper. Since it is (seemingly) sentient, I don't think the radio tower trick will last for long.

You know, the Russians were able to "contain" it in their communications room, likely due to analogue (non-electronic) radio sources. Or am I missing something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like the imagery of the ghost helicopter, but I can't bring myself to upvote the concept of Rotom as a skip.

The addendum is weird and interesting but the concept feels sort of convoluted to me, even for such a short article. I can't really identify what's wrong with the writing, but something is just keeping me from liking this.

Some of the initial reactions of the personnel operative to the thing seem unrealistic as far as my headcanon of Foundation procedures goes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this a lot. I was worries when i saw the photos, immediately thinking "autonomous rogue attack chopper", but this was really good. kind of cute.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well it's not that they haven't figured it out, it's just not explicitly stated in the main document.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I think it's good. I think it's a little obvious for the Foundation to not have figured it out, but it's a good Keter monster. I like monsters.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The firefly seems to be some kind of living energy construct thats looking for a vessel to contain itself. That or some kind of mobile, likely living EMP weapon.

Either way, it's an excellent article, No pointless "Lol redacted" or anything. +1.
